title: [navigation menu] Popup dimensions become stale when rapidly hovering between menu triggers

comments.body: I think this is kind of expected though as `keepMounted` popups preserve their state (this includes scroll position, uncontrolled form state — other components follow this). You don't need `keepMounted` on `Portal` if you want the content to be present in the SSR HTML, `Content` alone should suffice

I appreciate the response. I’m having a hard time seeing why preserving the state in a navigation menu like this is desirable, especially since it seems to be the trade-off for keeping the content mounted and visible to crawlers for SEO purposes. From a UX perspective, it feels a bit off, like something a user could interpret as a bug rather than intentional behavior.

Also, when using `keepMounted` only on the Content components, I’m seeing inconsistencies with submenu height transitions. If “Product” is the first top-level item hovered, the height transition doesn’t run properly until after hovering other top-level items.

--- 

Edit: I realize this can be worked around by controlling value/onValueChange at the root level (and coordinating top-level value state via React context), so it isn't a blocker. That said, it feels like more manual wiring than should be necessary just to reset submenu state in this scenario.

comments.body: BAS-425

Have you considered doing what [Apple's navigation menu](https://www.apple.com/) does instead: It shows a chevron trigger when using the keyboard but otherwise for pointer usage it remains hidden. This is a more accessible pattern than trying to hack on `onFocus` handlers. The `onFocus` technique to support a top-level link that's also a trigger has some issues: 1. A link that also triggers a submenu when it's focused mixes interaction models (navigate vs disclose) 2. On-focus opening is disorienting: tabbing across the navbar pops menus open just by landing on items, which is often discouraged (WCAG 3.2.1 "On Focus") especially for big popups like navigation menus 3. Screen reader browse vs focus mismatch: many SR users move a virtual cursor without real focus, so open on focus doesn't always fire and when it does, it can cause noisy/odd announcements 4. Adding `aria-haspopup/expanded` to an `` announces as “link” so “link, expanded” is confusing and implies conflicting primary actions 5. Focus-management edge cases (what you're hitting here): since the navigation menu popup is in a portal it uses focus guards to properly manage focus. In this case it's breaking due to the custom logic

Thank you for your response. The example you shared from Apple is intriguing, as well as your reasonings for why my proposed pattern might be more burdensome than helpful.

I would be interesting in learning how reimplementing Apple's strategy would work with the primitives from Base UI's NavigationMenu. It would seem that the link would still need to be wrapped fully in the `Trigger`, unless it wasn't and `onMouseEnter/onMouseLeave` handlers fired to managed the state. Additonally, just the arrow couldn't be a `Trigger` if you want the menu `Positioner` to `align="start"` with the entire item.

I will mess around with this some this evening, but I think it would be excellent if this could be documented within Base UI's NavigationMenu. Additionally, an example of this kind of approach and even updates to the component itself to make implementing it more straightforward would be greatly appreciated.

If I can get something working that follows the proposed pattern, I will update here.

> It would seem that the link would still need to be wrapped fully in the Trigger, unless it wasn't and onMouseEnter/onMouseLeave handlers fired to managed the state. Additonally, just the arrow couldn't be a Trigger if you want the menu Positioner to align="start" with the entire item.

Yeah, I haven't tried to do what Apple does with the Base UI navigation menu, but it seems like something we'd need to add new parts for to properly support without hacks on top (e.g. `NavigationMenu.TriggerLink`). I also tried to do this externally and it's not easy to add

Here's a [workaround for v1.3.0](https://stackblitz.com/edit/xshwf691) for the top-level link that opens a menu pattern (Apple-style), which requires splitting the trigger props to individual elements

Thank you so much for your example. I was able to use portions of it to make something work in a much more accessible fashion while maintaining our design pattern. Your work on the library, as well as your example, are much appreciated!

@atomiks With the example pattern you provided, I'm seeing the component get into weird states where `pointer-events: none` gets applied to the `body` tag and doesn't clear. I know your example is not an officially supported method of using the component, but I wanted to reach out to see if there was any known way to disable `pointer-events: none` from being applied to the `body` tag when a sub-menu is opened. I was looking for something like the `modal` prop used on Menus, or something similar, but I haven't found a lead that works.

Just to give some more context, it seems like there is a slight race condition when a menu is open/closing, and a route change is happening via Next router in our case. The soft-nav causes the `pointer-events` to stay on the `body` and not get cleared when a nav happens to happen at the exactly right time. It's very finicky.

I'll have to debug why that happens with the pattern

For now you should be able to set `pointer-events: initial; !important` on `<body>` in your CSS? Regular Menu submenus should no longer need it placed there

comments.body:
You can use `keepMounted` on `<NavigationMenu.Portal>`

Google and other web crawlers wait for hydration, so they'll see the content despite it not being in the initial HTML (due to the portal):

```jsx
<NavigationMenu.Portal keepMounted>
```

Thanks for the quick reply!

`keepMounted` only keeps the portal container. The actual items in the dropdown are only added to the DOM when I hover.

So it seems like `forceMount` in Radix fixes two things:

- the dropdown content is always available in the DOM, regardless of hover state (critical)
- the dropdown content is sent as HTML over the wire, so web crawlers don't need to wait for hydration (I agree this might not be a big deal at this day and age) (nice to have)

Curious if there's a solution that addresses both!

Yeah sorry, you're right

A relatively simple solution would likely be to render the children present in `<NavigationMenu.Content>` in a `<div hidden>` parent container which should solve both of those concerns. (You can also do this yourself externally as a workaround currently)
Thanks. Do you think support will be added in base-ui? if so, I'd rather hold off on any workarounds for now
Yeah, we'll fix this before the next release
@atomiks This would be super useful, is this still planned for the next release?

This fix will be available in the next npm release of Base UI.

In the meantime, you can try it out on our Canary release channel:

```sh
npm i https://pkg.pr.new/@base-ui/react@3794
```

comments.body:
I am experiencing the same issue. When moving the mouse quickly between navigation items, the `NavigationMenu.Content` component does not animate as expected.

Upon investigation, it appears that the `data-ending-style` attribute is not being applied when the mouse is moved rapidly. This may be the reason the transition behaves inconsistently.

This fix will be available in the next npm release of Base UI.

In the meantime, you can try it out on our Canary release channel:

```sh
npm i https://pkg.pr.new/@base-ui/react@4587
```

comments.body: I think `click-only` is technically possible at the moment by setting `delay`/`closeDelay` to really high values?

Right now, I went through the same route as I did for Radix (removing props for click). Funny enough, removing a 'ref' gives the best result.

And yes, setting delays would help with accidental hovering but not without drawbacks: when you click, you still feel that delay.

Another option would be controlling a nav menu manually and setting on click handlers.

> And yes, setting delays would help with accidental hovering but not without drawbacks: when you click, you still feel that delay.

I'm not sure how that's possible because the click handlers don't have any `delay` - when you click, it just opens instantly. Are you referring to the click occuring on `mouseup` not `mousedown`?

> Funny enough, removing a 'ref' gives the best result.

The hover listeners are assigned via the ref, not React props, which could explain this. But still - setting the delays to really high values does solve the issue since the hover open will just be delayed "forever" and cause it to never open on hover

In my testing, `delay` only applies to hovering and not to clicking. Still, I would like this feature too.

The Menu also has `closeOnClick` which is missing from Navigation Menu (but important if your nav menu is, for example, in your Next.js layout where its state persists when clicking a link).

Sorry for the confusion. When I said “you still feel that delay,” I was referring to my specific use case — I have a few triggers that should respond to click events, in addition to the existing hover-based triggers.

Essentially, I’d like both click and hover triggers to be active at the same time, but the delay is applied globally across the entire menu, which causes issues.

> Essentially, I’d like both click and hover triggers to be active at the same time

Ah right, your description made that clear in the original post already:

> some items should open on click only, while others can open on hover or click. Currently, NavigationMenu doesn't offer fine-grained control over interaction type per trigger

I guess `NavigationMenu.Item` could have a prop such as `openOnHover={false}`. Though, I don't think it should support hover-only, because keyboard users need a way to access the content. Are just `click-only` and `hover+click` enough to satisfy your use case?

> > Essentially, I’d like both click and hover triggers to be active at the same time
> 
> Ah right, your description made that clear in the original post already:
> 
> > some items should open on click only, while others can open on hover or click. Currently, NavigationMenu doesn't offer fine-grained control over interaction type per trigger
> 
> I guess `NavigationMenu.Item` could have a prop such as `openOnHover={false}`. Though, I don't think it should support hover-only, because keyboard users need a way to access the content. Are just `click-only` and `hover+click` enough to satisfy your use case?

Yes, absolutely!
It this planned to be added? Would be great to have this feature. Many nav setups on sites are click to open only. I have a need to do this as well.

> click to open only

You can use a really long open `delay` as a workaround, as that only applies to hover events. The workaround only doesn't work if there are "mixed" modes between triggers (which seems questionable UX-wise to me...)
You can also use `onMouseEnter={(e) => e.preventBaseUIHandler()}` on the `NavigationMenu.Trigger` as a workaround

comments.body:
Hey! 👋 Thanks for the detailed bug report — super helpful.

I ran into similar issues while working with dynamic content and nested menus, so I did some digging. Here are a few thoughts on possible workarounds/fixes:

1. Dynamic --popup-height 
   This can be handled by attaching a `ResizeObserver` to the `NavigationMenu.Content` element. That way, when the content changes (even conditionally or after async updates), we can recalculate and update the `--popup-height` variable to keep the transition smooth.

2. No transition on nested menus with defaultValue 
   This seems related to layout timing. A small delay using `requestAnimationFrame` or `setTimeout` before setting the default value can help give the DOM enough time to mount and animate properly.

3. Broken direction-based transitions without Popover/Positioner
   For custom layouts, it would be really helpful if direction state could be passed manually (or exposed via context), so we don’t rely strictly on `Popover` or `Positioner`. As a workaround, we can manually apply animation classes or styles based on direction logic.

Happy to help prototype one or more of these if needed — just let me know!

Thanks for offering to help! The main reason I’m posting these issues is that all of this works as expected with the Radix Navigation Menu out of the box, without requiring extra workarounds. I’m still using the Radix implementation for now, but I’m exploring ways to mitigate the gaps, so that later migration off the Radix will be easier not only for me but for others too.

These examples are very useful, thank you!

I think Radix avoids anchor positioning and uses CSS Grid, which has some drawbacks, but it ends up handling nested content transitions automatically. We also investigated CSS Grid for Collapsible but decided not to use it (for now): https://github.com/mui/base-ui/pull/1770

These should all be possible to fix without changing the positioning implementation, but I'll need to look into it

---

The key issue is that our implementation assumes you're creating a new popup when nesting `NavigationMenu` (like a `Menu` submenu), not inlining its viewport content inside of the root popup itself. This assumption essentially prevents nested inline content from working in most ways (most of the event handler logic/keyboard navigation fails to work correctly in your demo).

Allowing nested inlined content is likely going to take quite a bit of work and I don't have enough bandwidth atm to work on it (Combobox is taking priority), but I suppose fixing dot point 1 alone would allow custom implementations (i.e. custom event handlers to show/hide content inside the popup) as a workaround of sorts

This fix will be available in the next npm release of Base UI.

In the meantime, you can try it out on our Canary release channel:

```sh
npm i https://pkg.pr.new/@base-ui-components/react@2269
```
